name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَا لَكُمْ إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمُ ٱنفِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱثَّاقَلْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ أَرَضِيتُم بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ فَمَا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ (٣٨)
```

```english
O you who have faith! What is the matter with you that when you are told: ‘Go forth in the way of Allah,’ you sink heavily to the ground? Are you pleased with the life of this world instead of the Hereafter? But the wares of the life of this world compared with the Hereafter are but insignificant. (38)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا تَنفِرُوا۟ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا وَيَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ وَلَا تَضُرُّوهُ شَيْـًٔا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٣٩)
```

```english
If you do not go forth, He will punish you with a painful punishment, and replace you with another people, and you will not hurt Him in the least, and Allah has power over all things. (39)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا تَنصُرُوهُ فَقَدْ نَصَرَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِذْ أَخْرَجَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ثَانِىَ ٱثْنَيْنِ إِذْ هُمَا فِى ٱلْغَارِ إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصَـٰحِبِهِۦ لَا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَنَا ۖ فَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَيْهِ وَأَيَّدَهُۥ بِجُنُودٍ لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا وَجَعَلَ كَلِمَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلسُّفْلَىٰ ۗ وَكَلِمَةُ ٱللَّهِ هِىَ ٱلْعُلْيَا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٤٠)
```

```english
If you do not help him, then Allah certainly helped him when the faithless expelled him, as one of two [refugees], when the two of them were in the cave, he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us.’ Then Allah sent down His composure upon him, and strengthened him with hosts you did not see, and He made the word of the faithless the lowest; and the word of Allah is the highest; and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (40)
```

```arabic
ٱنفِرُوا۟ خِفَافًا وَثِقَالًا وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٤١)
```

```english
Go forth, whether [armed] lightly or heavily, and wage jihad with your possessions and persons in the way of Allah. That is better for you, should you know. (41)
```

```arabic
لَوْ كَانَ عَرَضًا قَرِيبًا وَسَفَرًا قَاصِدًا لَّٱتَّبَعُوكَ وَلَـٰكِنۢ بَعُدَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلشُّقَّةُ ۚ وَسَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَوِ ٱسْتَطَعْنَا لَخَرَجْنَا مَعَكُمْ يُهْلِكُونَ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (٤٢)
```

```english
Were it an accessible gain or a short journey, they would have surely followed you; but the distance seemed too far to them. Yet they will swear by Allah: ‘If we could, we would have surely gone forth with you.’ They [merely] destroy themselves. Allah knows that they are indeed liars. (42)
```

```arabic
عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنكَ لِمَ أَذِنتَ لَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكَ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ وَتَعْلَمَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٤٣)
```

```english
May Allah excuse you! Why did you grant them leave [to stay behind] before those who told the truth were evident to you and you had ascertained the liars? (43)
```

```arabic
لَا يَسْتَـْٔذِنُكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ أَن يُجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٤)
```

```english
Those who believe in Allah and the Last Day do not ask you for leave [exempting them] from waging jihad with their possessions and their persons, and Allah knows best the Godwary. (44)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا يَسْتَـْٔذِنُكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَٱرْتَابَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِى رَيْبِهِمْ يَتَرَدَّدُونَ (٤٥)
```

```english
Only those seek a leave [of exemption] from you who do not believe in Allah and the Last Day, and whose hearts are in doubt, so they waver in their doubt. (45)
```

```arabic
۞ وَلَوْ أَرَادُوا۟ ٱلْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا۟ لَهُۥ عُدَّةً وَلَـٰكِن كَرِهَ ٱللَّهُ ٱنۢبِعَاثَهُمْ فَثَبَّطَهُمْ وَقِيلَ ٱقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ (٤٦)
```

```english
Had they desired to go forth, they would have surely made some preparations for it; but Allah was averse to arouse them, so He held them back, and it was said [to them], ‘Be seated with those who sit back.’ (46)
```

```arabic
لَوْ خَرَجُوا۟ فِيكُم مَّا زَادُوكُمْ إِلَّا خَبَالًا وَلَأَوْضَعُوا۟ خِلَـٰلَكُمْ يَبْغُونَكُمُ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ وَفِيكُمْ سَمَّـٰعُونَ لَهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٧)
```

```english
Had they gone forth with you, they would have only added to your troubles, and they would have surely spread rumours in your midst, seeking to cause sedition among you. They have some spies among you, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers. (47)
```

```arabic
لَقَدِ ٱبْتَغَوُا۟ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ مِن قَبْلُ وَقَلَّبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأُمُورَ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَظَهَرَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَهُمْ كَـٰرِهُونَ (٤٨)
```

```english
They certainly sought to cause sedition earlier, and upset the matters for you, until the truth came and Allah’s command prevailed, much as they were averse. (48)
```

## (Commentary)

This section addresses the hypocrites (المنافقين), providing an exposition of their general characteristics and signs, and explaining the deception and plotting that Islam and the Muslims faced from them, as well as the calamities they endured due to their hypocrisy. Foremost among these is the reprimand of the believers for their sluggishness in striving (الجهاد), the account of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) departure from Mecca, and the mention of the Cave (الغار).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*O you who have faith! What is the matter with you that when you are told: ‘Go forth in the way of Allah,’ you sink heavily to the ground?*» (9:38). The word *iththaqaltum* (اثَّاقَلْتُمْ) is originally *tathaqaltum* (تثاقلتم), following the pattern of *iddaraku* (اداركوا) and similar forms. It seems to be imbued with the meaning of "inclining" or something similar, which is why it is followed by the preposition "to" (إلى). It is said that "you sank heavily to the ground" (اثاقلتم إلى الأرض) means you inclined toward the earth while being sluggish, or you were sluggish while inclining toward the earth. What is meant by "going forth in the way of Allah" (النفر في سبيل الله) is going out for striving (الجهاد).

And His saying: «*Are you pleased with the life of this world instead of the Hereafter?*» (9:38). The word "pleased" (الرضا) here seems to carry the meaning of "contentment" (القناعة), hence it is followed by the preposition "with/instead of" (من), just as one says: "I am content with the good part of the wealth" (رضيت من المال بطيبه), or "I am content with the friendship of so-and-so from among the people" (رضيت من القوم بخلة فلان). Based on this, there is a type of metaphorical nuance in the speech, as if the life of this world is a lowly form of the life of the Hereafter which they have accepted in its stead. This is suggested by His subsequent saying: «*But the enjoyment of the life of this world is but little in the Hereafter*» (9:38).

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: O you who have faith! What is the matter with you that when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—whose name is not explicitly mentioned out of protection and glorification—says to you: "Go out for striving," you are slow, as if you do not wish to go out? Have you become content with the life of this world, being pleased with it instead of the Hereafter? For the enjoyment of the life of this world, in comparison to the life of the Hereafter, is but little.

In this verse and those following it, there is a severe reprimand for the believers and a violent threat. It is applicable to the Expedition of Tabuk, as mentioned in the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*If you do not go forth, He will punish you with a painful punishment, and replace you with another people*» (9:39) to the end of the verse. The punishment they are warned of is absolute and unrestricted; there is no reason to restrict it to the punishment of the Hereafter. Rather, it remains in its ambiguity, and the context may support it being the punishment of this world, or the punishment of both this world and the Hereafter together.

And His saying: «*and replace you with another people*» (9:39) means He will substitute you with another people who will not be sluggish in complying with Allah’s commands and going forth in His way when they are told: "Go forth." The evidence for this meaning is the contextual indication (قرينة المقام).

And His saying: «*and you will not do Him any harm*» (9:39) is an indication of the insignificance of their affair to Allah, the Glorified, should He wish to take them away and bring others. For Allah does not benefit from them; rather, their benefit is for themselves, and thus their harm is upon themselves. His saying: «*and Allah has power over all things*» (9:39) is the justification for His saying: «*He will punish you with a painful punishment, and replace you with another people.*»

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*If you do not help him, Allah has already helped him when the faithless expelled him, as one of two [persons], when the two were in the cave*» (9:40). "One of two" (ثاني اثنين) means one of them. The **Cave** (الغار) is a large hole in a mountain. What is meant here is the cave of Mount Thawr near Mina, which is different from the Cave of Hira where the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to seek refuge before the commencement of his mission (البعثة), according to widespread reports (الأخبار المستفيضة). The "companion" (صاحبه) mentioned is Abu Bakr, according to definitive transmission (النقل القطعي).

And His saying: «*when he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us’*» (9:40) means: do not grieve out of fear from the loneliness, the estrangement, the lack of helpers, and the enemies' alliance and pursuit of me, for Allah, the Glorified, is with us, helping me against them.

And His saying: «*Then Allah sent down His composure upon him and strengthened him with hosts you did not see*» (9:40) means: Allah sent down His composure (سكينته) — *the divine tranquility and peace that steadies the heart* — upon His Messenger and strengthened His Messenger with hosts you did not see, who diverted the people from them through various means of diversion, using all the factors that worked to turn the people away from entering the cave and capturing him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Various things have been narrated regarding this, which will be mentioned in the "Discussion on Traditions," Allah the Exalted willing.

The evidence that the pronoun in His saying: «*Then Allah sent down His composure upon him*» refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is, firstly: the return of the pronouns before and after it to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), such as His saying: «*If you do not help him*» (تَنْصُرُوهُ), «*helped him*» (نَصَرَهُ), «*expelled him*» (أَخْرَجَهُ), «*he said*» (يَقُولُ), «*to his companion*» (لِصاحِبِهِ), and «*strengthened him*» (أَيَّدَهُ). There is no way for the pronoun "upon him" (عَلَيْهِ) among them to refer alone to someone else without a definitive contextual clue (قرينة قاطعة) indicating so.

Secondly: the discourse in the verse is directed toward explaining Allah’s help for His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at a time when no one was with him who was capable of helping him, as He says: «*If you do not help him, Allah has already helped him when...*» The sending down of composure and the strengthening with hosts are forms of help, so that belongs to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) specifically.

This is further indicated by the repetition of "when" (إِذْ), mentioned three times in the verse, each being an exposition of what preceded it in some way. His saying: «*when the faithless expelled him*» is an exposition of the time of His saying: «*Allah has already helped him.*» His saying: «*when the two were in the cave*» is an exposition specifying the state mentioned in His saying: «*as one of two.*» And His saying: «*when he said to his companion*» is an exposition specifying the time indicated by His saying: «*when the two were in the cave.*»

Thirdly: the verse follows a single context until it says: «*and He made the word of the faithless the lowest, while the word of Allah is the highest*» (9:40). There is no doubt that this is an exposition of what preceded it, and that what is meant by "the word of the faithless" is what they decided upon in the *Dar al-Nadwa* and their resolve to kill him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and extinguish the light of Allah. "The word of Allah" is what He promised him of victory and the completion of His light. How could it be permissible to separate the exposition from the thing being expounded, making the exposition refer to His help for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), while the thing being expounded refers to His help for someone else?

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: If you do not help him, O believers, then Allah has already manifested His help for him at a time when he had no one to help him or defend him, while the enemies had allied against him and surrounded him from every side. That was when the polytheists plotted against him and resolved to kill him, so he was forced to leave Mecca in a state where he was but one of two men. That was when the two were in the cave, when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to his companion—who was Abu Bakr—: "Do not grieve over the situation you see; Allah is with us, and in His hand is victory." So Allah helped him.

He did so by sending down His composure upon him and strengthening him with hosts hidden from your eyes, and He made the word of the faithless—which was their judgment that he must be killed and their resolve upon it—a defeated word, neither effective nor influential. And the word of Allah—which is the promise of victory, the manifestation of the religion (الدين), and the completion of the light—is the highest, the exalted, the dominant. And Allah is All-Mighty (عزيز), never overcome, All-Wise (حكيم), never ignorant nor mistaken in what He wills and does.

From what has preceded, it has become clear, firstly: that His saying: «*Then Allah sent down His composure upon him*» is branched from His saying: «*Allah has already helped him,*» just as it is branched from His saying: «*when he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve.’*» For the time-frame is the time-frame of the help, as mentioned, and the discourse is directed toward explaining His help for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and no one else. Thus, the branching is upon the time-frame and its content, which is His saying: «*Allah has already helped him,*» not upon His saying: «*he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve.’*»

It has sometimes been argued that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was always in a state of composure from his Lord, so the sending down of composure in this specific circumstance reveals that it was sent down upon his companion.

This is refuted, firstly: by His saying, the Exalted: «*Then Allah sent down His composure upon His Messenger and upon the believers*» (9:26) in the story of Hunayn. The claim that his noble soul was somewhat agitated during the Battle of Hunayn, making the descent of composure appropriate there unlike the situation in the cave, is refuted by the fact that it is a fabrication without knowledge. The verse does not mention any grief or agitation or anything else from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), except what it mentions regarding the flight of the believers. Furthermore, it invalidates the original argument that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was always in a state of composure from his Lord such that nothing new could occur for him; how then was it permissible for him to be agitated at Hunayn so that a new composure descended upon him? Unless they mean that he was only always in that state while in the cave.

A similar case is the verse speaking of the descent of composure upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and upon the believers in Surah al-Fath: «*When the faithless harboured in their hearts a zealotry, the zealotry of [the Age of] Ignorance, then Allah sent down His composure upon His Messenger and upon the believers*» (48:26).

It is refuted, secondly: by the necessity of His saying: «*and strengthened him with hosts you did not see*» being branched immediately after His saying: «*Then Allah sent down His composure upon him,*» because they are in the same context. This would necessitate that the strengthening with hosts also did not refer to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), or it would require a break in the single context without any justifying reason.

Some have committed themselves—to escape the disgrace of a broken context—to the view that the pronoun in His saying: «*and strengthened him*» also refers to his companion. This would necessitate that the sending down of composure and the strengthening with hosts both return to Abu Bakr rather than the Prophet (ص).

Others have supported this by saying that the events in which the verses mention the descent of hosts they did not see, such as the Battle of Hunayn and the Trench (الأحزاب), as well as the descent of angels for the Battle of Badr—even if they do not explicitly mention their descent upon the believers or state their strengthening by them—since they only descended for victory, and in that is the victory of the believers and their reinforcement, there is no barrier to saying that the hosts they did not see only strengthened Abu Bakr. Their strengthening of all the believers, or Abu Bakr specifically, is in reality a strengthening of the Prophet (ص).

Based on this explanation, it would be better to make the third branch, which is His saying: «*and He made the word of the faithless the lowest*» to the end of the verse, dependent on the two preceding branches so that the context is not broken.

It should not be hidden from you that what they have committed to takes the verse out of its stable, unified meaning into a self-contradictory meaning where its end refutes its beginning and its conclusion negates its opening. For the verse began by stating that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is more honored by Allah and more dear than to be humiliated or made in need of the help of these people; rather, He, the Exalted, is his Guardian (الولي) who stands by his help when none of those surrounding him or following his tracks were present. Then, when it begins to explain His help for him, it explains His help for someone else by sending down composure upon him and strengthening him with hosts they did not see, until the end of the verse.

Even if we grant that His help for some of the believers in him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or all of them is in reality help for him, the verse is in a context that absolutely refutes this. For the previous verse addresses the believers in a single address—«*O you who have faith!*»—and reprimands and threatens them for being sluggish in responding to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding what he commanded them of going forth in the way of Allah and going out for striving. Then the second verse threatens them with punishment and replacement if they do not go forth, and explains to them that Allah and His Messenger are in no need of them and they will not harm Him in the least. Then the third verse clarifies that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is in no need of their help because his Lord is his Guardian and Helper, and He helped him when none of them had any part in it—and that was His help for him when the faithless expelled him as one of two, when the two were in the cave, when he said to his companion: "Do not grieve; Allah is with us."

It is clear and beyond doubt that the requirement of this context is to explain His help for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) specifically, pertaining to his person from Allah, the Glorified, alone, without any role for any of the believers in that. It is not to explain His help for him through the believers or some of them, when He has just gathered them in an address of reprimand, nor to explain His help for some of the believers who were with him.

Nor is the context suitable for His saying: «*when the faithless expelled him, as one of two*» to be a brief indication of the dear help for His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and then to begin detailing the specific distinction granted to his companion by the descent of composure and strengthening with hosts. For the context, as has been clarified to you, rejects that.

It is refuted, thirdly: that in this there is a neglect of the true meaning of composure (السكينة), the discussion of which has already preceded under His saying: «*Then Allah sent down His composure upon His Messenger and upon the believers*» (9:26).

**The second matter:** What is meant by strengthening him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with hosts they did not see is his strengthening by that on that very day, as the context implies. As for the saying of some that what is meant is the strengthening with hosts on the Day of the Trench and the Day of Hunayn as stated in other verses, there is absolutely no evidence for this from the wording.

**The third matter:** What is meant by "the word" (الكلمة) in His saying: «*and He made the word of the faithless the lowest*» is what they decided in the *Dar al-Nadwa* and their resolve to kill him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and thereby invalidate his true call. By His saying: «*while the word of Allah is the highest,*» it is what Allah promised His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of victory and the manifestation of His religion over all religions.

This is because this verse, with what it contains of His saying: «*Allah has already helped him when the faithless expelled him,*» refers to what is recounted in His saying: «*When the faithless plotted against you to take you captive, or to kill you, or to expel you. They plotted and Allah plotted, and Allah is the best of plotters*» (8:30). That which is at the end of the verse regarding the invalidation of their word and the realization of the divine word is linked to what is at the beginning of the verse regarding the account of expulsion—meaning the necessity of being forced to leave. What forced him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to leave was their resolve to kill him according to the judgment they agreed upon to kill him. This is the word that Allah, the Glorified, invalidated and made the lowest, and the word of Allah stands in opposition to it, which is nothing but victory and manifestation.

From here it becomes clear that the saying of some—that what is meant by the word of the faithless is polytheism (الشرك) and disbelief (الكفر), and by the word of Allah, the Exalted, is monotheism (التوحيد) and faith (الإيمان)—is not sound. For although polytheism is a word of theirs and monotheism is a word of Allah, it does not necessitate that they are what is meant every time the two words are mentioned, especially when a contextual clue exists to the contrary.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*Go forth, light and heavy, and wage jihad with your possessions and your persons in the way of Allah. That is better for you, should you know*» (9:41). "Light" (خفافاً) and "heavy" (ثقالاً) are the plurals of light and heavy. "Heaviness," by the indication of the context, is a metaphor for the presence of distracting obstacles that turn a person away from going out for striving, such as numerous financial preoccupations, love for family, children, relatives, and friends which makes parting from them disliked, or the lack of provisions, a mount, weapons, and the like. "Lightness" is a metaphor for the opposite of that.

Thus, the command to go forth light and heavy—which are two opposite states—carries the meaning of the command to go out in any state, and not to take any of those things as an excuse to justify abandoning the departure. Similarly, the combination of possessions and persons in the mention carries the meaning of the command to strive by any means possible.

It has thus become clear that the command in the verse is absolute and does not reject being restricted by excuses that waive the obligation of striving, such as illness, blindness, lameness, and the like. For what is meant by lightness and heaviness is something beyond that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*Had it been an accessible gain and a short journey, they would have followed you*» (9:42) to the end of the verse. "Gain" (العرض) is that which quickly vanishes, and it is applied to worldly wealth, which is what is meant in the verse by the indication of the context. What is meant by its being "accessible" (قريباً) is its being easy to obtain. "Short" (القاصد) comes from *qasd*, which is moderation in an affair; what is meant by the journey being "short" is that its destination is not far and it is easy for the traveler. "Distance" (الشقة) refers to the distance because of the hardship (*mashaqqa*) involved in traversing it.

The verse, as appears from its context, is a reproach and censure of the hypocrites who stayed behind and did not go out with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for striving in the Expedition of Tabuk. For the expedition in which the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out and the hypocrites stayed behind, and which involved a long distance, was the Expedition of Tabuk and no other.

The meaning of the verse is: Had what you commanded them and called them to been an easily obtainable gain, a present booty, and a short, easy journey, they would have followed you, O Muhammad, and gone out with you out of greed for the booty. But the distance and the journey seemed long to them, so they found the travel difficult and were sluggish in it.

And they will swear by Allah when you return to them and blame them for staying behind: "Had we been able to go out, we would have gone out with you," destroying their own souls by the path they took: of going out to fight when they hoped for worldly gain if it was easy to seize, and staying behind when it was difficult for them, then making false excuses to their Prophet and swearing by Allah in that while lying. Or they destroy their souls by this false oath, and Allah knows that they are indeed liars.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*May Allah excuse you! Why did you grant them leave [to stay behind] until those who spoke the truth were evident to you and you had known the liars?*» (9:43). The first sentence is a prayer for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for excuse, similar to the prayer against man for being killed in His saying: «*Perish man! How ungrateful he is!*» (80:17), and His saying: «*so let him be perished! How he decided!*» (74:19), and His saying: «*May Allah assail them! Where are they being diverted?*» (9:30).

The sentence is connected to His saying: «*Why did you grant them leave?*»—meaning in staying behind and sitting back. Since the question is for disapproval or reproach, its meaning is: it was appropriate that you should not have granted them leave to stay behind and sit back. Through this, the connection of the objective contained in His saying: «*until those who spoke the truth were evident to you*» to His saying: «*Why did you grant them leave?*» becomes sound. The connection is to the thing being questioned, not the question itself; otherwise, it would convey the opposite of what is intended. The discourse is directed toward explaining the manifestation of their lying and that the slightest test, such as refraining from granting them leave to sit back, would reveal their true nature.

The meaning of the verse is: May Allah excuse you! Why did you grant them leave to stay behind and sit back? Had you wished, you would not have granted them leave—and they were more deserving of that—until those who spoke the truth were evident to you and you had known the liars, so that their lying and hypocrisy would be distinguished by you.

The verse—as you see and as was previously indicated—is in the position of claiming the manifestation of their lying and hypocrisy and that they are disgraced by the slightest test they are put through. Among the features of this position is directing the reprimand to the addressee, reproaching him and disapproving of him, as if he were the one who covered up their disgraceful acts and evil inner state. This is a type of rhetorical nuance through which the manifestation and clarity of the matter is explained; nothing more than that is intended. It is among the categories of exposition by way of: "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor."

Thus, what is intended by the speech is to manifest this claim, not to reveal a shortcoming of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or a poor management on his part in reviving the cause of Allah, or his committing a sin thereby—far be it from him. The "priority" of not granting them leave means that not granting leave was more suitable for the manifestation of their disgrace and that they were more deserving of that due to their evil inner state and corruption of intention, not because it was more appropriate or better in itself or closer to the interest of the religion.

The evidence for what we have mentioned is His saying, the Exalted, three verses later: «*Had they gone forth with you, they would have only added to your confusion and would have scurried about in your midst, seeking to cause sedition among you, and among you are some who would listen to them*» (9:47). Thus, it was more beneficial to grant them leave to stay behind to protect the collective from confusion, corruption of opinion, and the splitting of the word. It was necessary that they sit back so they would not tempt the believers by sowing discord among them and causing sedition, for among them were those weak in faith and diseased in heart who would listen to them and hasten to obey them. Had they not been granted leave and then manifested their opposition, the sedition would have been more severe and the split in the collective word more obvious and clear.

This is confirmed by His saying, the Exalted, two verses later: «*Had they desired to go forth, they would have made some preparation for it, but Allah was averse to their setting out, so He held them back, and it was said, ‘Stay behind with those who stay behind’*» (9:46). Their staying behind and hypocrisy were apparent and manifest from their lack of preparation, which every person of intellect could discern in their faces. Such a thing would not be hidden from the likes of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), whom Allah had repeatedly informed of their news before the revelation of this Surah. How then could it be correct to reprimand him here with a serious reprimand as to why he did not refrain from granting leave and did not ascertain their state until their hypocrisy became evident to him and he distinguished the hypocrites from the believers? Thus, nothing is intended by the reprimand except what we have mentioned.

From what has preceded, the falsity of the saying of those who said that the verse indicates the occurrence of a sin from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) becomes clear—because excuse (العفو) is not realized without a sin—and that the granting of leave was ugly on the part of him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and among the minor sins because it is not said regarding a permissible act: "Why did you do it?" End quote.

This is part of their playing with the speech of Allah, the Glorified. If an objector were to object to what they themselves compose in a similar context to that in which the verse was delivered, they would not be pleased with it. We have clarified that the verse is delivered for a purpose other than the purpose of serious reprimand.

Furthermore, their saying that regarding a permissible act it is not said: "Why did you do it?" is false. For it is permissible, when someone is seen preferring the non-optimal over the optimal, to say to him: "Why did you do that and prefer it over what is more optimal than it?" Moreover, you have already known that the verse is not delivered for a serious reprimand.

Similar to this is what another mentioned, saying: "Some commentators, especially al-Zamakhshari, have behaved with poor etiquette in expressing Allah’s excuse for His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in this verse. They should have learned the highest etiquette with him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as his Lord and Educator informed him of the excuse before the sin, which is the ultimate honoring and kindness."

Others, like al-Razi, went to the other extreme, wanting to prove that excuse does not indicate a sin, and at most, the granting of leave for which Allah reprimanded him was contrary to the optimal (خلاف الأولى).

This is a stagnation with modern technical terms and specific custom regarding the meaning of "sin" (الذنب) as being "disobedience" (المعصية). They should not have fled from affirming what Allah affirmed in His Book by clinging to their technical terms and custom which contradict it and contradict the linguistic meaning as well.

**Sin** (الذنب) in the language is every act that entails harm or the loss of a benefit or interest, derived from the "tail" (*dhanab*) of an animal, and it is not synonymous with "disobedience" but is more general than it. The excused leave-granting entailed the loss of the interest stipulated in the verse, which is the manifestation of those who spoke the truth and the knowledge of the liars. And He, the Exalted, has said: «*Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory, that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come*» (48:1-2).

Then he mentioned in a long discourse that this was an exertion of judgment (اجتهاد) from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in matters where there was no revelation from Allah, and this is permissible and has occurred from the Prophets (peace be upon them), and they are not infallible from error in it. Infallibility (العصمة) — *the divine protection from error and sin* — upon which there is agreement is specific to the delivery of the revelation, its exposition, and acting upon it. Thus, it is impossible for the Messenger to lie or err in what he delivers from his Lord or to contradict it by action.

Among this is what preceded in Surah al-Anfal regarding the reprimand of the Exalted for His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in taking ransom from the prisoners of Badr, where He said: «*A prophet may not take captives until he has decimated [the enemy] in the land. You desire the transitory goods of this world, while Allah desires the Hereafter*» (8:67). Then He explained that it necessitated the descent of a painful punishment were it not for a prior decree from Allah that acted as a barrier. End of his summarized discourse.

I wonder what he added in his discourse to what al-Razi and others escaped to, when they mentioned that it was from the "abandonment of the optimal" and they do not call it a sin in the custom of the practitioners of the Sharia—which is that which entails punishment—while he mentioned that it was from the "abandonment of the most beneficial" and called it a sin linguistically.

Furthermore, you have already known from what preceded that it was not a sin, neither customarily nor linguistically, by the explicit indication of the verses that their not going out was what was most beneficial for the state of the Muslim army, to save them thereby from the calamity of sedition occurring and the splitting of the word. This very reason was present even if the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had not granted them leave and they had manifested what they harbored of disbelief and opposition. And what Allah mentioned by His saying: «*Had they desired to go forth, they would have made some preparation for it*»—that their lack of preparation indicated their lack of desire to go forth—the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was too great for that to be hidden from him while they were within his sight and hearing.

In addition, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to recognize them by the tone of their speech, as the Exalted said: «*and you will surely recognize them by the tone of [their] speech*» (47:30). How could it be hidden from one who heard from one of them such as his saying: «*Give me leave and do not put me into temptation*» (9:49), or who says of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): «*He is [all] ear*» (9:61), or who slanders him regarding the charities, and who is not sincere toward him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), that these are the precursors of hypocrisy appearing from them, and behind them is nothing but disbelief and opposition?

Thus, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to discern hypocrisy and opposition from them and knew what was in their souls. Despite that, the reprimand of him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) as to why he did not refrain from granting leave and did not ascertain their state and did not distinguish them from others is nothing but a non-serious reprimand for the purpose we mentioned.

As for his saying: "The excused leave-granting entailed the loss of the interest stipulated in the verse, which is the manifestation of those who spoke the truth and the knowledge of the liars," the response is that the interest contained in the verse is the manifestation of those who spoke the truth *to the Prophet* (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and *his* knowledge of the liars, not absolute manifestation nor absolute knowledge of the liars. It has appeared from what preceded that this was not hidden from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and that the true interest was indeed in the granting of leave, which was to close the door of sedition and the splitting of the word. For he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) knew from their state that they were absolutely not going out, whether he granted them leave to sit back or did not. So he hastened to grant leave to preserve the appearance of obedience and the unity of the word.

It is not for you to imagine that had their hypocrisy been made evident that day and their opposition appeared by the Prophet not granting them leave to sit back, the people would have been saved from their sedition and their sowing of discord, because of the power and strength Islam possessed that day—which was the day of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) departure for the Expedition of Tabuk—and the influence of his word.

For Islam that day only possessed strength and awe in the eyes of the people from among the non-Muslims, who were terrified by its power, magnified the numbers of its people, and feared the edge of their swords. As for the Muslims within their own society and among themselves, they had not yet become free from hypocrisy and the disease of the hearts, nor had the unity of the word and the seriousness of purpose and resolve yet taken complete hold of them. The evidence for this is these very verses and those that follow them until nearly the end of the Surah.

They had manifested similar behavior on the Day of Uhud when the enemy attacked them in the heart of their territory, and a third of the Muslim army returned from the battlefield, and neither exhortation nor insistence affected them, until they said: "Had we known there would be fighting, we would have followed you." That was one of the active causes in the defeat of the Muslims.

As for his saying: "And among His reprimand for His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for his error in his judgment is what preceded in Surah al-Anfal regarding the reprimand for taking ransom from the prisoners of Badr, where He said: ‘A prophet may not take captives until he has decimated [the enemy] in the land...’"

The response is, firstly: that this is from poor understanding. For it is clear and beyond doubt that the verse, by its wording, does not reprimand for taking ransom from the prisoners, but rather reprimands for the very act of taking prisoners—"A prophet may not take captives." No verse was revealed nor has any narration been reported stating that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had commanded them to take captives. Rather, the narrations of the story indicate that when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded the killing of some prisoners, the people feared he would kill them all, so they spoke to him and insisted on taking ransom from them to strengthen themselves thereby against the enemies of the religion. Allah rejected this from them by His saying: «*You desire the transitory goods of this world, while Allah desires the Hereafter.*»

This is among the best witnesses that the reprimand in the verse is directed toward the believers specifically, without the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being specific to it or sharing it with them, and that most of the reports that have come regarding this meaning are fabricated or interpolated.

Secondly: that the reprimand in the verse, even if it were specific to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or included him and others, would not be from the reprimand for what he mentioned as "sin" in its linguistic sense—which is the loss of interest in some way. For this reprimand is concluded by the saying of the Exalted in the following verse: «*Had it not been for a prior decree from Allah, there would have surely befallen you a great punishment for what you took*» (8:68). No person of intellect can doubt that the threat of a "great punishment" is only possible when the thing threatened is from "disobedience" (المعصية) in its technical sense, and indeed from the major sins. This is also among the witnesses that the reprimand in the verse is directed toward someone other than the Prophet (ص).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*Those who believe in Allah and the Last Day do not ask you for leave*» (9:44) to the end of the two verses. The two verses mention one of the ways by which the hypocrite is recognized and distinguished from the believer, which is asking for leave to stay behind from striving in the way of Allah.

Allah, the Glorified, has explained that striving in the way of Allah with possessions and persons is among the necessities of faith in Allah and the Last Day in the reality of faith, because of the attribute of godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah that protects one from His displeasure* — that this faith bequeaths. Since the believer possesses godwariness by virtue of faith in Allah and the Last Day, he possesses insight regarding the obligation of striving in the way of Allah with his wealth and his person. This does not allow him to be sluggish in it such that he would ask for leave to sit back. But the hypocrite, due to the lack of faith in Allah and the Last Day, has lost the attribute of godwariness, so his heart has become filled with doubt, and he continues to waver in doubt, preferring to stay on the sidelines and asking for leave to stay behind and sit back from striving.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*Had they desired to go forth, they would have made some preparation for it*» (9:46) to the end of the verse. **Preparation** (العدة) is the readiness. **Setting out** (الانبعاث)—according to *al-Majma‘*—is to set off quickly in an affair. **Holding back** (التثبيط) is to stop someone from an affair by making them lose interest in it.

The verse is coordinated with what preceded of His saying: «*and Allah knows that they are indeed liars*» according to the meaning—that is, they are liars in their claim of being unable to go out; rather, they did not desire it. Had they desired it, they would have made some preparation for it, because among the signs of one who desires an affair is that he readies himself for it with the appropriate preparation and readiness, and nothing of that appeared from them.

And His saying: «*but Allah was averse to their setting out, so He held them back*» (9:46) means: as a recompense for their hypocrisy and as a favor upon you and upon the believers, so that they would not corrupt your collective and split your word by sedition and sowing discord.

And His saying: «*and it was said, ‘Stay behind with those who stay behind’*» (9:46) is a non-legislative command that does not contradict the legislative command to go forth and go out. For Allah commanded them through the tongue of His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to go forth and go out—which is a legislative command—and He commanded them from the side of their corrupt inner state and the doubt wavering in their hearts and their evil internal dispositions to sit back—which is a non-legislative command—and there is no contradiction between them.

He did not attribute the saying: «*Stay behind with those who stay behind*» to Himself, to sanctify Himself from commanding what He does not approve of, and there are intermediate causes commanding that, such as the Devil and the self (النفس). It is only attributed to the Exalted through an intermediary because the meaning of recompense and favor upon the believers applies to it.

And so that the two commands, which differ in form, may agree in the context—namely His saying: «*you were told: ‘Go forth in the way of Allah’*» and His saying: «*it was said, ‘Stay behind with those who stay behind.’*»

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*Had they gone forth with you, they would have only added to your confusion and would have scurried about in your midst*» (9:47). **Confusion** (الخبال) is corruption and the agitation of opinion. **Scurrying** (الإيضاح) is to hasten in evil. **Midst** (الخلال) is "between." **Seeking** (البغي) is the request; thus the meaning of "seeking to cause sedition among you" is that they request sedition for you or among you, as has been said. **Sedition** (الفتنة) is the trial, such as division and the splitting of the word, according to the meanings of it that suit the verse. **Listening** (السماع) is to be quick in responding and accepting.

The verse is in the position of justifying His saying: «*but Allah was averse to their setting out, so He held them back*» as a favor; therefore, it is brought as a separate sentence without coordination. The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*They have certainly sought to cause sedition before, and they upset the matters for you until the truth came and Allah’s command became manifest, while they were averse*» (9:48) means: I swear, they have certainly sought trial, the splitting of the word, and the division of the collective before this expedition—which is the Expedition of Tabuk—as in the Battle of Uhud when Abd Allah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul returned with a third of the people and deserted the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). And they upset the matters for you by calling the people to opposition, inciting them to disobedience, deserting them from striving, and urging the Jews and the polytheists to fight the believers, and spying, and other things, until the truth came—which is the truth that must be followed—and Allah’s command became manifest—which is what He desires of the religion—while they were averse to all of that.

The verse bears witness to the previous verse by mentioning examples, just as an affair is proven by its like. The direction of the address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) specifically, after its generality in the previous verse, is because the matter in it is specific to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—namely, the upsetting of matters for him—unlike what is in the previous verse regarding their going out among the people.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «*If you do not help him, Allah has already helped him*» (9:40): Ibn Mardawayh and Abu Nu‘aym in *al-Dala’il* narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out by night, he reached the cave of Thawr." He said: "And Abu Bakr followed him. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) heard his movement behind him, he feared it was the pursuit. When Abu Bakr saw that, he cleared his throat. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) heard that, he recognized him. So he stood for him until he followed him, and they reached the cave."

"In the morning, Quraysh went out in pursuit of him. They sent for a man from the trackers of Banu Mudlij, who followed the tracks until he reached the cave. At its door was a tree, and the tracker urinated at its base. Then he said: 'Your companion whom you seek did not pass this place.' He said: 'At that moment, Abu Bakr grieved.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him: **لا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَنا** — 'Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us.'"

He said: "He and Abu Bakr remained in the cave for three days. Amir ibn Fuhayra would bring them food, and Ali was preparing them. They bought three camels from the camels of Bahrain and hired a guide for them. When some of the third night had passed, Ali brought them the camels and the guide. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) rode his mount, and Abu Bakr rode another, and they headed toward Medina, while Quraysh had sent out in pursuit of him."

In the same source, Ibn Sa‘d narrated from Ibn Abbas, Ali, Aisha bint Abi Bakr, Aisha bint Qudama, and Suraqa ibn Ju‘shum—the narration of some of them entering into others—they said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out while the people were sitting at his door. He took a handful of pebbles and began to scatter them over their heads while reciting: **يس وَالْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ** — 'Ya Sin. By the wise Quran' (36:1-2) and the following verses, and he passed by."

"Someone said to them: 'What are you waiting for?' They said: 'Muhammad.' He said: 'By Allah, he has already passed you.' They said: 'By Allah, we did not see him.' They stood up, shaking the dust from their heads. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and Abu Bakr went to the cave of Thawr and entered it. A spider spun its webs over its door, one over the other."

"Quraysh sought him with the utmost pursuit until they reached the door of the cave. Some of them said: 'There is a spider over it from before the birth of Muhammad.'"

In *I‘lam al-Wara*, regarding the account of Suraqa ibn Ju‘shum with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he said: "What became famous among the Arabs, which they recite in poetry and discuss in their homes, is that he followed him while he was heading toward Medina, seeking to catch him off guard (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to gain favor with Quraysh. When the opportunity presented itself to him and he was certain he had captured his quarry, the legs of his horse sank until they were entirely hidden in the ground, and he was in a barren place and a level plain. He knew that what had afflicted him was a heavenly matter. He called out: 'O Muhammad! Pray to your Lord to release my horse for me, and by the covenant of Allah, I will not lead anyone to you.' So he prayed for him, and his horse leaped up as if it had been released from a tether. He was a shrewd man and knew from what he saw that there would be great news for him. He said: 'Write for me a guarantee of safety.' So he wrote for him, and he departed."

Muhammad ibn Ishaq said: "Abu Jahl said some verses regarding the matter of Suraqa, and Suraqa answered him in verse:

*O Aba al-Hakam, by al-Lat, had you been a witness*
*To the matter of my horse when its legs sank,*
*You would have wondered and not doubted that Muhammad*
*Is a Prophet with a proof; so who can oppose him?*
*It is upon you to restrain the people from him, for I*
*See that his matter will one day have its signs manifest.*"

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Kafi* with its chain from Mu‘awiya ibn Ammar from Abu Abd Allah (peace be upon him), and in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through several paths, and al-Zamakhshari mentioned it in *Rabi‘ al-Abrar*.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Sa‘d and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Mus‘ab, who said: "I met Anas ibn Malik, Zayd ibn Arqam, and al-Mughira ibn Shu‘ba, and I heard them narrating: 'On the night of the cave, Allah commanded a tree, and it grew in the face of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and covered him. And Allah commanded the spider, and it spun in the face of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and covered him. And Allah commanded two wild pigeons, and they stood at the mouth of the cave.'"

"The youths of Quraysh approached, a man from every clan, with their sticks, swords, and clubs. When they were about forty cubits from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), one of them hurried and looked into the cave, then returned to his companions. They said: 'What is the matter with you that you did not look into the cave?' He said: 'I saw two pigeons at the mouth of the cave, so I knew there was no one in it.'" The tradition continues.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from al-Zuhri regarding His saying: «*when the two were in the cave*» (9:40), he said: "The cave that is in the mountain called Thawr."

**I say:** The narrations are widespread that the cave mentioned in the Noble Quran is the cave of Mount Thawr, which is approximately four leagues from Mecca.

In *I‘lam al-Wara* and *Qisas al-Anbiya’*: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained in the cave for three days. Then Allah the Exalted gave him permission for the migration (الهجرة) and said: 'Leave Mecca, O Muhammad, for you have no helper in it after Abu Talib.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out."

"A shepherd for some of Quraysh, called Ibn Urayqit, approached. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called him and said: 'O Ibn Urayqit, can I entrust you with my life?' He said: 'Then, by Allah, I will guard you and protect you and not lead anyone to you. Where do you intend, O Muhammad?' He said: 'Yathrib.' He said: 'I will surely take you on a path where no one can find their way.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him: 'Go to Ali and give him the glad tidings that Allah has permitted me to migrate, so let him prepare provisions and a mount for me.'"

"And Abu Bakr said to him: 'Go to Asma’, my daughter, and tell her: Prepare provisions and two mounts for me. And inform Amir ibn Fuhayra of our matter'—he was among the freed slaves of Abu Bakr and had embraced Islam—'and tell him: Bring us the provisions and the two mounts.'"

"So Ibn Urayqit came to Ali (peace be upon him) and informed him of that. Ali ibn Abi Talib sent provisions and a mount to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and Ibn Fuhayra sent provisions and two mounts. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out from the cave, and Ibn Urayqit took him on the path of Nakhla between the mountains. They did not return to the road until Qudayd, where they stayed with Umm Ma‘bad."

He said: "The Ansar had received news of the Messenger of Allah’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) departure to them, and they were expecting his arrival until he reached the Mosque of Quba’ and stayed there. The men and women went out, rejoicing at his arrival."

**I say:** The reports regarding the details of the stories of the migration are extremely numerous, narrated by the masters of transmission and the authors of biographies from both the Shi‘a and the Sunnis. Despite their number, they are conflicting and agitated; this book does not have the scope to critique them and extract the pure from them. For the indication of the general story, what we have provided is sufficient, and it is as if it is agreed upon among the reports of both groups.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Khaythama ibn Sulayman al-Tarabulsi in *Fada’il al-Sahaba* and Ibn Asakir narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: "Allah censured all the people and praised Abu Bakr, saying: «*If you do not help him, Allah has already helped him—when the faithless expelled him, as one of two, when the two were in the cave—when he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us’*»."

**I say:** A critical examination of the contents of the verses surrounding the story and what is joined to them of sound transmission necessitates a poor opinion of this narration. For the verses that censure the believers—or all the people as in the narration—and to which the verse of the Cave refers, including His saying: «*If you do not help him,*» are His saying, the Exalted: «*O you who have faith! What is the matter with you that when you are told: ‘Go forth in the way of Allah,’ you sink heavily to the ground?*» (9:38). Definitive transmission indicates that the mentioned sluggishness was not from the generality of the believers and all of them, and that many of them hastened to respond to the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in what he commanded of going forth. Rather, a group of people, both believers and hypocrites, were sluggish.

Thus, the address «*O you who have faith!*» which includes all the believers, and the censure following it, is only from the address to a group regarding some of them, like the address to the Jews in His saying: «*Why then did you kill the prophets of Allah?*» (2:91) and others. This is frequent in the Quran, except that the habit of the Quran in such instances is not to waste the right of the righteous nor the reward of the virtuous—meaning the few who are included in such general addresses of censure and reproach. So their matter is rectified, and they are excepted and mentioned with beauty, as He did in what will follow in this Surah of verses praising the believers and thanking their beautiful efforts, in His saying: «*But the faithful, men and women, are comrades of one another*» (9:71), and others.

If the verses—having been revealed regarding the Expedition of Tabuk—include all the believers, those hastening to go out and those sluggish in it without exception, then they include the generality of the companions and the believers, and among them was Abu Bakr himself, except that He, the Exalted, rectified what had attached to those hastening in obedience and response among them in subsequent verses and thanked their effort.

So if His saying in the verse: «*If you do not help him*» refers to what preceded of the account—

The reluctance to mobilize (التثاقل) and the allusion to it as a reproach to all the people would include a reproach of Abu Bakr just as it is a reproach of others for failing to aid the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or for their sluggishness in supporting him. Furthermore, the verse does not allow for an indication that Abu Bakr aided him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), given His saying: **فَقَدْ نَصَرَهُ اللهُ إِذْ أَخْرَجَهُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا ثانِيَ اثْنَيْنِ إِذْ هُما فِي الْغارِ إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصاحِبِهِ لا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَنا** — "Allah certainly helped him when the faithless expelled him, as one of two [refugees], when the two were in the cave, and he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us.’" (9:40). Rather, if it indicates anything, it indicates the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) aid to Abu Bakr, as he comforted his heart and consoled him by saying: "Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us."

Moreover, you have already recognized in the previous commentary (البيان) that the verse, by virtue of its context, only addresses the aid of Allah, the Glorified, alone to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) specifically in his own person, in contrast to the assumed lack of aid from the entirety of the believers and their desertion of him. Thus, the indication of the verse that on the day of the cave, none aided the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) except Allah, the Glorified, alone is a definitive indication (دلالة قطعية).

This meaning is itself the clearest witness that the pronouns in the remainder of the verse’s sentences: **فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ وَأَيَّدَهُ بِجُنُودٍ لَمْ تَرَوْها وَجَعَلَ كَلِمَةَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا السُّفْلى وَكَلِمَةُ اللهِ هِيَ الْعُلْيا** — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon him, and strengthened him with hosts you did not see, and He made the word of the faithless the lowest; and the word of Allah is the highest" (9:40), refer to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). These sentences are structured to explain His, the Exalted, sole undertaking of aiding him with a mighty, unseen aid in which no human had any part—namely, the sending down of composure (السكينة) upon him, strengthening him with hosts hidden from sight, making the word of the disbelievers the lowest, and exalting the word of Truth; and Allah is All-Mighty, All-Wise.

As for merits other than the aiding of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for which a person might be praised, if there were anything of that sort here, it would be in His saying: "one of two (ثانِيَ اثْنَيْنِ)" and in His saying: "to his companion (لِصاحِبِهِ)." Let us concede that a person being the second of two, one of whom is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and his being a companion to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) mentioned in the Quran with companionship (الصحبة), are among the honors that people aspire to. However, these are social merits (مناقب اجتماعية) whose value and preciousness are estimated within societies. As for the Noble Quran, it has another criterion for value, and in its logic, excellence and nobility have another meaning resting upon a reality higher than conventional social objectives—namely, the dignity of servitude (كرامة العبودية) and the degrees of proximity (القرب) and nearness (الزلفى).

Mere physical companionship (الصحابة الجسمانية) and inclusion in a count do not indicate any of that. It has been repeated in His, the Exalted, speech that being called by various names or being clothed in what the general public aspires to and what social perception deems great has no value with Allah, the Glorified. Rather, the reckoning is based on what is in the hearts, not what appears from outward deeds or the precedence of prestige (الأحساب) and lineage (الأنساب).

He has expressed this most eloquently regarding the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his close associates specifically, in His, the Exalted, saying: **مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ تَراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً** — "Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating..." until He says: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً** — "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward" (48:29). Look at the praise in the beginning of the verse and the qualification (القيد) at its end, and reflect.

This is a brief portion of what pertains to the verse and the narrations in this discussion; anything beyond this amount would take us out of exegetical discussion (البحث التفسيري) into theological discussion (البحث الكلامي), which is outside our purpose.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il*, and Ibn Asakir in his *Tarikh* narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: **فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ** — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon him" (9:40), that he said: "Upon Abu Bakr, because the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) always had composure with him."

In the same source, al-Khatib narrated in his *Tarikh* from Habib ibn Abi Thabit regarding: **فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ** — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon him," that he said: "Upon Abu Bakr; as for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), composure was already upon him."

**I say:** It has been established previously that the pronoun refers back to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) according to what the context (السياق) guides to. The two narrations, despite their suspended chains (الوقف), are weak, and the word of Ibn Abbas or Habib is not a binding proof (حجية) for others.

As for the argument they presented in them—that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) always had composure with him—it is flawed (مدخولة) and refuted by His, the Exalted, saying in the story of Hunayn: **ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the faithful" (9:26). Similar to it is the verse in Surah al-Fath referring to the story of al-Hudaybiyyah; both explicitly state the descent of composure upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in those specific instances. Thus, the matter should follow the same pattern in the cave.

Some of them (1) sensed the problem and interpreted their statement in the two narrations—that composure never left the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—to mean something else: that composure was inseparable from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the cave, which would then serve as a contextual clue that the composure which descended there only descended upon his companion and not him. Perhaps Habib’s narration is closer in its indication to what he mentioned.

He said, after citing the narration of Ibn Abbas and then the narration of Habib: "Some commentators of language and reason have adopted this narration and clarified the reasoning within it—that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not experience any agitation (اضطراب), fear, or grief at that time. Some strengthened it by the principle that the default for a pronoun is to return to the nearest mentioned noun (أقرب مذكور). But this is nothing."

Others held that the pronoun returns to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and that the descent of composure upon him does not necessitate that he was fearful, agitated, or distressed. This [view] is weak because the descent of composure is coordinated (عطف) with what preceded it, indicating its occurrence after it and its being consequential to it, and that its descent occurred after his saying to his companion: "Do not grieve." [End of quote].

As for what they mentioned regarding the absence of fear or agitation occurring to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at that time: if they derived this from the lack of mention of any such thing in the verse or in a reliable narration, then His, the Exalted, speech in the stories of Hunayn and al-Hudaybiyyah is also devoid of mentioning the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with fear, grief, or agitation, and no reliable narration has been transmitted indicating that. So how was the mention of the descent of composure upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sound in those two instances?

If they say that the descent of composure necessitates [prior] agitation, fear, and grief, this is rejected, as previously mentioned. How so? The descent of one of the divine blessings (النعم الإلهية) does not depend on being previously characterized by an opposite state or a corresponding affliction, just like the descent of mercy after mercy, blessing after blessing, and faith and guidance after faith and guidance, and so on. The Noble Quran has explicitly stated many matters of this kind.

As for his saying: "That the return of the pronoun to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is weak because the coordination of the descent of composure with what preceded it indicates its occurrence after it and its being consequential to it, and that its descent occurred after his saying to his companion: 'Do not grieve,'"

The response is: There is no doubt that the "fa" of derivation (فاء التفريع) indicates the consequence of what follows it upon what precedes it and its occurrence after it, but this is a sequentiality of rank (بعدية رتبية), not a sequentiality of time (بعدية زمانية), and no one has said it must always be temporal.

It is necessary in our case that His saying: **فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ وَأَيَّدَهُ** — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon him, and strengthened him" (9:40) be consequential to what preceded it of the discourse, not to what is nearest to it over others, except according to the view that the default for a pronoun is to return to the nearest mentioned noun—a view he himself weakened in his earlier remarks.

What is suitable from what preceded to be the basis for the aforementioned derivation is His saying: "Allah certainly helped him (فقد نصره الله)" in such-and-such a time. The derivation of these branches from it is like the derivation of the detailed (التفصيل) from the general (الإجمال). The context remains consistent: "Allah certainly helped him at such a time, so He sent down His composure upon him, strengthened him with hosts you did not see, and made the word of the faithless the lowest."

Thus, it becomes clear that what he finally answered with is the very thing he initially weakened regarding the principle of the proximity of the referent to the pronoun—that principle which has no basis—he repeated it a second time by merely changing the wording.

From here, the grounds for criticizing another narration become clear, which was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Mardawayh from Anas ibn Malik: "He said: The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and Abu Bakr entered the cave of Hira—so Abu Bakr said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'If one of them were to look at the place of his feet, he would see me and you.' He said: 'What is your thought of two, of whom Allah is the third? Indeed, Allah has sent down His composure upon you and strengthened me with hosts you did not see.'"

Furthermore, the narration mentions the cave of Hira (غار حراء), while it has been established by widely transmitted and abundant reports (المستفيض المتكاثر) that the cave was the cave of Thawr (غار ثور), not the cave of Hira.

Moreover, the narration explicitly involves a fragmentation of the context (تفكيك السياق) by its saying: "sent down His composure upon you and strengthened me with hosts," etc.

Al-Alusi in *Ruh al-Ma'ani* cited the narration as follows: "Indeed, Allah sent down His composure upon you and strengthened you with hosts you did not see," thus returning both pronouns to Abu Bakr instead of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

We do not know which of the two wordings is the original and which is the distorted one, except that to the narration "and strengthened you with hosts you did not see," in addition to the aforementioned problems, other problems are added that were mentioned in the previous commentary, along with a new problem regarding His saying: "you did not see (لم تروها)" using the plural address (خطاب الجمع), while there was no plural audience at that time.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His, the Exalted, saying: **لَوْ كانَ عَرَضاً قَرِيباً وَسَفَراً قاصِداً** — "Had it been a near gain and a short journey" (9:42): In a narration by Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **لَوْ كانَ عَرَضاً قَرِيباً وَسَفَراً قاصِداً**, he says: "A near spoil (غنيمة قريبة), **لَاتَّبَعُوكَ** — 'they would have surely followed you.'"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them) regarding the saying of Allah: **لَوْ كانَ عَرَضاً قَرِيباً وَسَفَراً قاصِداً لَاتَّبَعُوكَ** — "Had it been a near gain and a short journey, they would have surely followed you" (9:42). The verse [continues]—"They are able, and it was in the knowledge of Allah—that had it been a near gain and a short journey, they would have done so."

**I say:** Al-Saduq narrated it in *al-Ma'ani* with his chain from Abd al-Ala ibn A'yan from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) similarly.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His, the Exalted, saying: **وَلكِنْ بَعُدَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الشُّقَّةُ** — "but the distance seemed too far to them" (9:42), meaning to Tabuk. The reason for this was that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) never traveled a journey further than it nor more difficult than it.

The cause of this was that summer traders used to come to Medina from Syria with flour (الدرموك) and food. They were Nabataeans, and they spread rumors in Medina that the Romans had gathered, intending to raid the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with a massive army, and that Heraclius had marched with the collection of his troops, bringing with them Ghassan, Judham, Bahra, and Amilah. His vanguards had reached al-Balqa, while he himself had stopped at Homs.

So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent his companions to Tabuk, which is in the lands of al-Balqa, and sent word to the tribes around him, to Mecca, and to those who had accepted Islam from Khuza'ah, Muzaynah, and Juhaynah, urging them to Jihad.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ordered his camp to be pitched at Thaniyyat al-Wada', and he ordered the people of wealth (أهل الجدة) to assist those who had no strength. Whoever had anything brought it out; they carried [others], strengthened [them], and urged one another toward that.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) delivered a sermon and said, after praising Allah and extolling Him: "O people, indeed the most truthful of speech is the Book of Allah. The most worthy of words is the word of God-consciousness (كلمة التقوى). The best of religions is the religion of Abraham. The best of traditions is the tradition of Muhammad. The most noble of speech is the remembrance of Allah. The best of stories is this Quran. The best of matters are those determined with resolve (عزائمها), and the worst of matters are innovations (محدثاتها). The best of guidance is the guidance of the Prophets. The most noble of the slain are the martyrs. The blindest of blindness is wandering after guidance. The best of deeds is that which benefits. The best of guidance is that which is followed. The worst of blindness is the blindness of the heart. The upper hand is better than the lower hand. That which is little and sufficient is better than that which is much and distracting. The worst of excuses is at the presence of death. The worst of regrets is on the Day of Resurrection. Among the people is he who does not come to the Friday prayer except rarely (نزرا). Among them is he who does not remember Allah except with nonsensical speech (هجرا). Among the greatest of sins is the lying tongue. The best of wealth is the wealth of the soul (غنى النفس). The best of provisions is God-consciousness (التقوى). The head of wisdom is the fear of Allah. The best of what is cast into the heart is certainty (اليقين). Doubt (الارتياب) is from disbelief (الكفر). Distancing oneself from the deeds of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). Embezzlement (الغلول) is from the pus of Hell. Intoxication is a coal of the Fire. Poetry is from Iblis. Wine is the gatherer of sin. Women are the snares of Iblis. Youth is a branch of madness. The worst of earnings is the earning of usury (الربا). The worst of eating is eating the property of the orphan. The fortunate (السعيد) is he who takes warning from others. The wretched (الشقي) is he who is wretched in his mother's womb. Each of you shall eventually go to a place of four cubits. The matter is to its end, and the criterion of a matter is its conclusion. The most excessive usury is lying. Everything that is coming is near. Reviling a believer is transgression (فسوق). Fighting a believer is disbelief (كفر). Eating his flesh is from the disobedience of Allah. The sanctity of his property is like the sanctity of his blood. Whoever relies on Allah, He suffices him. Whoever is patient triumphs. Whoever pardons, Allah pardons him. Whoever restrains his rage, Allah rewards him. Whoever is patient in the face of calamity, Allah compensates him. Whoever seeks reputation (السمعة), Allah will expose him. Whoever fasts, Allah multiplies [the reward] for him. Whoever disobeys Allah, He punishes him. O Allah, forgive me and my nation. O Allah, forgive me and my nation. I seek Allah's forgiveness for me and for you."

He said: So the people desired Jihad when they heard this from the Messenger of Allah. The Arab tribes whom he had summoned arrived, while a group of hypocrites and others stayed behind. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) met al-Jadd ibn Qays and said to him: "O Abu Wahb, will you not march with us in this raid? Perhaps you might take some of the daughters of the 'Yellow Ones' (بنات الأصفر) [the Romans]." He said: "O Messenger of Allah, by Allah, my people know that there is no one among them more captivated by women than I. I fear that if I go out with you, I will not be able to remain patient when I see the daughters of the 'Yellow Ones.' So do not tempt me, and permit me to stay." And he said to a group of his people: "Do not go out in the heat."

His son said: "You talk back to the Messenger of Allah and say to him what you say, then you say to your people: 'Do not march in the heat'? By Allah, Allah will surely send down a Quran regarding this that people will read until the Day of Resurrection." So Allah sent down to His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding that: **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ ائْذَنْ لِي وَلا تَفْتِنِّي ـ أَلا فِي الْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ** — "Among them is he who says, ‘Give me leave, and do not put me into ordeal.’ Behold! They have already fallen into ordeal, and indeed hell besieges the faithless." (9:49).

Then al-Jadd ibn Qays said: "Does Muhammad hope that the war with the Romans is like the war with others? Not one of these will ever return."

**I say:** These meanings have been narrated in many other reports from both Shi'i and Sunni paths.

In *al-Uyun*, with its chain from Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm, who said: "I attended the assembly of al-Ma'mun while al-Rida Ali ibn Musa (peace be upon him) was with him. He said to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, is it not your view that the Prophets are infallible (معصومون)?' He said: 'Yes.' So al-Ma'mun said to him—among what he asked—'O Abu al-Hasan, tell me about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **عَفَا اللهُ عَنْكَ لِمَ أَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ** — "May Allah excuse you! Why did you give them leave..." (9:43).'"

Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: "This is among what was revealed as: 'I mean you, but listen, O neighbor' (إياك أعني واسمعي يا جارة) — *a rhetorical device where the speech is addressed to one person but intended for others*. Allah, the Exalted, addressed His Prophet with that but intended his nation. Likewise is His, the Mighty and Majestic, saying: **لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ** — 'If you ascribe a partner [to Allah], your deeds will surely go for nothing, and you will surely be among the losers' (39:65), and His, the Exalted, saying: **وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَدَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً** — 'Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly been on the verge of inclining toward them a little' (17:74)." He said: "You have spoken the truth, O son of the Messenger of Allah."

**I say:** The content of the narration aligns with what we presented in the explanation of the verse, contrary to what they mentioned—that his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) giving them leave to stay behind was of the nature of "leaving the better option" (ترك الأولى), for the verse would not be consistent with being of the type "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor" in that case.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abd al-Razzaq narrated in *al-Musannaf*, and Ibn Jarir from Amr ibn Maymun al-Awdi, who said: "There were two things the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did regarding which he had not been commanded anything: his giving leave to the hypocrites, and his taking [ransom] from the captives. So Allah sent down: **عَفَا اللهُ عَنْكَ لِمَ أَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ** — 'May Allah excuse you! Why did you give them leave...'" (9:43).

**I say:** The discussion on the content of this narration has already preceded.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His, the Exalted, saying: **وَلَوْ أَرادُوا الْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا لَهُ عُدَّةً** — "Had they desired to go forth, they would have surely prepared some equipment for it" (9:46), and what follows it, he said: "There remained behind the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) people of [sincere] intentions and insights (بصائر) who were not overtaken by doubt or suspicion, but they said: 'We shall catch up with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).'"

Among them was Abu Khaythama, who was strong and had two wives and two shaded arbors (عريشان).
