His two wives had sprinkled his arbors [with water to cool them], cooled water for him, and prepared food for him. He looked out over his arbors, and when he saw them, he said: "No, by Allah, this is not fair! The Messenger of Allah—Allah has forgiven him his past and future sins—has gone out in the heat and wind, carrying weapons and striving in the way of Allah, while Abu Khaythama is strong, sitting in his arbor with two beautiful women? No, by Allah, this is not fair!"

Then he took his she-camel, fastened his saddle upon her, and caught up with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The people saw a rider on the road and informed the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of that. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Let it be Abu Khaythama." He approached and informed the Prophet of what had happened with him, so he rewarded him with good and prayed for him.

Abu Dharr had stayed behind the Messenger of Allah for three days because his camel was emaciated. He caught up after three days, but his camel stopped on the way. So he left it, carried his belongings on his back, and when the day brightened, the Muslims saw a person approaching. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Let it be Abu Dharr." They said: "It is Abu Dharr." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Reach him, for he is thirsty." So they reached him with water.

Abu Dharr reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while he had a water-skin (إداوة) containing water. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O Abu Dharr, you have water with you and yet you were thirsty?" He said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah—may my father and mother be sacrificed for you—I came upon a rock with rainwater on it. I tasted it and found it sweet and cool, so I said: 'I will not drink it until the Messenger of Allah drinks it.'"

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O Abu Dharr, may Allah have mercy on you. You live alone, you die alone, you are resurrected alone, and you enter Paradise alone. A group of people from the people of Iraq will be fortunate because of you—they will undertake your washing, your preparation, the prayer over you, and your burial."

Then he said: "There had stayed behind the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) a group of hypocrites, and a group of insightful believers (مؤمنين مستبصرين) in whom no hypocrisy was found: among them were Ka'b ibn Malik the poet, Murarah ibn al-Rabi', and Hilal ibn Umayyah al-Rafi'i. When Allah accepted their repentance, Ka'b said: 'I was never stronger than I was at that time when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out to Tabuk. I never had two mounts gathered for me except on that day. I used to say: I will go out tomorrow or the day after, for I am strong. I procrastinated and felt sluggish for days after the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had left. I would enter the market and not fulfill any need. Then I met Hilal ibn Umayyah and Murarah ibn al-Rabi', who had also stayed behind. We agreed to go early to the market, but we fulfilled no need. We kept saying: We will go out tomorrow and the day after, until news reached us of the Messenger of Allah's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) return, and we regretted it.'"

"When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrived, we met him to congratulate him on his safety. We greeted him, but he did not return our greeting and turned away from us. We greeted our brothers, but they did not return our greeting. News of that reached our families, so they cut off speech with us. We would attend the mosque, but no one would greet us or speak to us. Our wives came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'We have heard of your displeasure with our husbands; should we separate from them?' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Do not separate from them, but let them not come near you.'"

"When Ka'b ibn Malik and his two companions saw what had befallen them, they said: 'What keeps us in Medina while the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), our brothers, and our families do not speak to us? Come, let us go out to this mountain and remain there until Allah accepts our repentance or we die.'"

"So they went out to Dhubab—a mountain in Medina. They would fast, and their families would bring them food, placing it aside and then turning away from them without speaking to them."

"They remained like this for many days, weeping night and day and calling upon Allah to forgive them. When the matter became long for them, Ka'b said to them: 'O people, Allah and His Messenger are displeased with us, and our families and brothers are displeased with us and do not speak to us—so why are we not displeased with one another?' So they separated on the mountain and swore that none of them would speak to his companion until he died or Allah accepted his repentance. They remained like that for three days, each one of them in a part of the mountain, none of them seeing his companion or speaking to him."

"On the third night, while the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in the house of Umm Salamah, their repentance was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **لقد تاب الله بالنبي على المهاجرين والأنصار ـ الذين اتبعوه في ساعة العسرة** — 'Allah has certainly turned [graciously] to the Prophet and the Emigrants and the Helpers who followed him in the hour of difficulty' (9:117). Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: 'This is how it was revealed, and they are Abu Dharr, Abu Khaythama, and Umayr ibn Wahb—those who stayed behind then caught up with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).'"

"Then He said regarding those three: **وَعَلَى الثَّلاثَةِ الَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا** — 'And to the three who were left behind' (9:118). The Knower (peace be upon him) said: 'It was only revealed: "upon the three who opposed (خالفوا)"; for if they had been "left behind" (خُلِّفُوا), there would have been no blame upon them. **حَتَّى إِذا ضاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ** — "until the earth became narrow for them despite its expanse" (9:118), as the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), their brothers, and their families would not speak to them, so Medina became narrow for them until they left it. **وَضاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ** — "and their own souls became narrow for them" (9:118), as they swore not to speak to one another, so they separated, and Allah accepted their repentance when He recognized the sincerity of their intentions.'"

**I say:** The discussion regarding these two verses and the narrations concerning them will follow.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from al-Mughirah, who said: "I heard him saying regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَلَوْ أَرادُوا الْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا لَهُ عُدَّةً** — 'Had they desired to go forth, they would have surely prepared some equipment for it' (9:46). He said: 'He means by equipment (العدة) the intention (النية); He says: "Had they an intention, they would have gone forth."'"

**I say:** This narration, despite its weakness, its being disconnected (إرسال), and its being attributed to an unnamed Imam (إضمار), does not align with the wording of the verse. Allah knows best.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Ishaq and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from al-Hasan al-Basri, who said: "Abdullah ibn Ubayy, Abdullah ibn Nabtal, and Rifa'ah ibn Zayd ibn Tabut were among the leaders of the hypocrites, and they were among those who plotted against Islam and its people. Regarding them, Allah revealed: **لَقَدِ ابْتَغَوُا الْفِتْنَةَ مِنْ قَبْلُ ـ وَقَلَّبُوا لَكَ الْأُمُورَ** — 'They have certainly sought to cause sedition before, and they upset the matters for you' (9:48) until the end of the verse."
